sanskrit: कर्षयन्तः शरीरस्थं भूतग्राममचेतसः । मां चैवान्तःशरीरस्थं तान्विद्ध्यासुरनिश्चयान् ॥
hindiTranslation: जो मनुष्य शास्त्रविधिसे रहित घोर तप करते हैं जो दम्भ और अहङ्कारसे अच्छी तरह युक्त हैं जो भोगपदार्थ, आसक्ति और हठसे युक्त हैं जो शरीरमें स्थित पाँच भूतोंको अर्थात् पाञ्चभौतिक शरीरको तथा अन्तःकरणमें स्थित मुझ परमात्माको भी कृश करनेवाले हैं उन अज्ञानियोंको तू आसुर निश्चयवाले आसुरी सम्पदावाले समझ ॥
englishTranslation: Those who are ignorant and perform terrible austerities without scriptural guidance, wasting away the body’s elements and Me dwelling within the body, should be known as having demonic resolve.
essence: Harshness is not holiness when it destroys the body and ignores what lives within.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: Blind, self-willed austerity can damage the body and ignore the divine presence within it. Such harsh practice is not purity; it comes from confused, destructive resolve.
simpleInsight: Not every hard practice is noble. Discipline without wisdom can become self-harm.
detailedMeaning: Krishna is still answering Arjuna on the battlefield, where every choice feels life-and-death. After describing misguided worship and harsh austerity, he sharpens the warning: some people even weaken their own body and ignore the divine presence within it. That is not true discipline.
modernRelevance: Someone brags about fasting through exhaustion, sleeping three hours, and calling it commitment. The body is breaking, the mind is proud, and the practice is feeding ego instead of clarity.
translationLiteral: कर्षयन्तः (wasting away) / शरीरस्थम् (dwelling in the body) / भूतग्रामम् (the group of elements / body) / अचेतसः (the unthinking) / माम् (Me) / च (and) / एव (also) / अन्तःशरीरस्थम् (dwelling within the body) / तान् (them) / विद्धि (know) / आसुरनिश्चयान् (having demonic resolve)
insight: It shifts discipline from self-punishment to wise restraint that protects both body and intention.
